As I settled into my role in the military, there was an underlying tension in the world that we all felt. We knew that the situation in Korea could escalate at any moment, and Europe was no different. The atmosphere was charged with a sense of rivalry and suspicion, as nations eyed each other warily, each side building up its defenses and stockpiling weapons. Our unit was always prepared for action, ready to respond to any provocation. Despite the uncertainty, I found my time in active duty to be some of the happiest years of my life. It was peacetime, and while nobody was shooting at me, the camaraderie among my fellow soldiers was palpable. Everyone was congenial and friendly, united by a shared purpose, and we took our jobs seriously, especially the officers and the dedicated enlisted men who were career army personnel, committed to maintaining stability in a world on edge.

text: In 1968, my unit was called to go to Vietnam, and I was appointed as the officer in charge of the security police for a prominent air wing known for its strategic operations and vital support roles. We had everything prepared for deployment, and I had even arranged for my family to rotate visits while I was stationed at Wright-Patterson Air Force Base, a hub for advanced military logistics and training. However, the night before I was set to leave, I received a call from Colonel Holbert, who informed me that I wouldn’t need to go to active duty because we had enough security personnel already deployed. I was relieved and felt no bitterness about the situation; I understood how quickly things could change.

Throughout my time in England, I forged lasting friendships with the guys in my outfit. We’ve been having reunions since 1974, and the last one was right here in Chattanooga. I even had the privilege of buying caps for each of our veterans, which proudly display the 752nd Engineers emblem. Our first reunion in a charming town known for its rich music heritage and southern hospitality had about 150 people, including wives. Over the years, we’ve gathered in various locations like Kingsport, Nashville, Memphis, Gatlinburg, and even a city in Ohio where our company commander hailed from. The warmth of the people and the beautiful rolling hills always make our gatherings special. We share a strong camaraderie, and it’s always a joy to reconnect with old friends. Their presence was a morale booster, and it felt good to have a taste of home, even if just for a little while. I can still picture Bob Hope’s humor and the way he connected with the troops. It was a welcome break from the tension of our daily lives.

We were out on an outpost when the MPs arrived, and I immediately felt a sense of unease. They were searching for someone, and I knew that MPs don’t come to the front lines unless it’s serious. When they called out his name, I held my breath, wondering if they would call mine next. Thankfully, they didn’t. They took him away, explaining that his mother had sent a letter stating he was too young. He was barely out of his teens, with a boyish face that still held traces of innocence, and he often fumbled with his gear, a sign of inexperience. He protested, claiming she was lying, but they had proof—his birth certificate. They took him off, leaving me behind, and I felt a mix of relief and concern.

I remember the day vividly, a moment that would change everything. I was just 15 years old, sitting in our apartment in Manhattan, listening to the radio. The symphony was playing, and then suddenly, the broadcast was interrupted. The announcer's voice cut through the music, delivering the shocking news that the Japanese had bombed a naval base in the Pacific, a surprise attack that would thrust our nation into war. It felt surreal, like something out of a movie. My parents, having lived through the previous world war and the tumultuous events leading up to the conflict in Europe, had sensed that we might soon be drawn into another global struggle. They were right, but I was still too young to grasp the full weight of what this meant for my future. The atmosphere was thick with uncertainty, and the world felt like it was on the brink of chaos.
origin: I remember the day vividly, December 7th, 1941. I was just 15 years old, sitting in our apartment in Manhattan, listening to the radio. The symphony was playing, and then suddenly, the broadcast was interrupted. The announcer's voice cut through the music, delivering the shocking news that the Japanese had bombed Pearl Harbor. It felt surreal, like something out of a movie. My parents, having lived through World War I and the tumultuous events leading up to the war in Europe, had sensed that we might soon be drawn into conflict. They were right, but I was still too young to grasp the full weight of what this meant for my future.

We were the first replacements for a unit that had recently faced an ambush, a group renowned for their bravery and tenacity in combat. I was issued my rifle and assigned to the third squad of the third platoon. I quickly learned that we were stepping in for men who had been caught off guard just days before, a unit that had endured heavy fighting and had earned a reputation for their grit. The winter was brutal, and the cold was relentless.

I remember when the auto plants were humming, and you could hear the rhythm of the machines all the way from the east side. Everybody had a job, and a good one at that, building those land yachts. It was a real boomtown back then, full of promise.

Back then, it was a quiet spa town, a place for gentle folk to take the waters. I remember strolling past the Pittville Pump Room, the architecture so grand, so very British. It always felt a world away from the fighting I'd seen.

The air was thick with smoke and the smell of something burning, acrid in my nostrils. I remember the silence in that hall, heavy with the weight of what had been done. Even now, the faces of the accused sometimes flicker in my dreams.

After my time in Korea, I returned to Fort Leonard Wood for a brief period before being assigned to a base in Abilene, Texas, known for its expansive runways and a strong focus on air mobility operations. There, I worked on an intriguing project: constructing an experimental runway. This wasn’t just any runway; it was made of aluminum panels, each about 2-by-8 feet, that locked together to create a runway stretching about a thousand feet long. I remember the day I decided to dedicate my life to helping others. As a child, I was always the one running around the neighborhood, rescuing fallen birds and tending to my friends when they scraped their knees. I found joy in caring for those in need, whether it was a simple cut or a little bird that required assistance. When the Great Depression hit and my father lost everything, my mother suggested a path that involved service and compassion. It seemed like a perfect fit, so we explored our options. The best institution in New York State at the time was Saint Francis Hospital in Poughkeepsie, which was a bit of a trek from our home on Long Island, but we had a summer house nearby. I applied, got accepted, and spent three wonderful years there, learning the art of healing and the science of care. I immersed myself in understanding the human body, mastering the skills of observation, and developing the ability to comfort those in distress. I learned to administer treatments, manage medications, and provide emotional support to patients and their families. Under the guidance of the black Franciscans, a highly educated order of nuns, I grew in my ability to advocate for those who were vulnerable and to bring hope to those in pain.

As the conflict in Europe came to an end, I was ready to return home. I had gathered enough points for discharge after enduring years of hardship, camaraderie, and the relentless march of soldiers across foreign lands. I arrived back in Cleveland on June 26, 1945, welcomed by my parents at the train station, their faces a mix of relief and joy. Although tensions were still high in the Pacific, with battles raging and lives at stake, I felt grateful to be home, prepared to embark on a new chapter in my life. The benefits I would receive would support my college education, and I was determined to make the most of it, inspired by my experiences in the trenches, the sacrifices of my fellow service members, and the hope for a brighter future in a world striving for peace.

One of my most memorable experiences during our monthly training exercises occurred while living on a small island like Guam, where we regularly faced the threat of tornadoes and hurricanes. Despite severe weather warnings, we had to volunteer to move logistical supplies outside to the tarmac where the aircraft were parked, preparing for missions that required precision and teamwork. It didn’t matter if a storm was brewing; we had to brave the elements, ensuring that everything was in place for takeoff. Those moments were tough, filled with the roar of engines and the urgency of our tasks, but they forged a strong bond among us as we worked together under pressure, united by our commitment to serve and protect.

The salt air always smelled the same, no matter which landing craft I was on. We were all just kids, really, heading towards that gray strip of sand and praying. I still see those boys in my dreams sometimes, forever young on that beach.

The smell of Gauloises and fresh bread still takes me back. I remember the cobblestone streets, slick with rain, reflecting the warm glow of the cafes. Even now, I can almost hear the accordions playing those mournful, beautiful tunes.

When we finally arrived in Vietnam, I remember the journey vividly. After a couple of stops, we landed in Guam, but it wasn’t long before we flew straight into a remote area surrounded by lush greenery and rugged terrain, where our hospital was set to be established. The moment I stepped off the plane, I was hit with a culture shock. The air was heavy, thick with humidity and an unfamiliar smell that was overwhelming, mingling with the scent of tropical vegetation and distant smoke. Yet, I didn’t feel dread like many of my fellow soldiers. Instead, I approached it as an adventure, albeit a naive one. I could hear the distant sounds of military activity and the chatter of locals, and I knew there was a lot happening around me, but I didn’t fully grasp the severity of the situation I was stepping into.

text: We were out on an outpost when the MPs arrived, and I felt a wave of anxiety wash over me. They were looking for a young soldier, and I knew that MPs don’t come to the front lines unless it’s serious. When they called out his name, I held my breath, wondering if they would call mine next. Thankfully, they didn’t. They took him away, explaining that his mother had sent a letter stating he was underage. He protested, claiming she was lying, but they had official records with his date of birth clearly printed, along with the seal of the issuing authority. They took him off, leaving me behind, and I felt a mix of relief and concern.

They took him off, leaving me behind, and I felt a mix of relief and concern.

Eventually, we arrived in Le Havre, France, and boarded freight trains designed to carry a large number of soldiers or a few horses, with cramped compartments that left little room to move. The cars were made of sturdy metal, built for durability rather than comfort, and the smell of oil and rust filled the air. We were packed in tightly, and after a long journey, we were unloaded in Belgium. There, we were taken to a chateau on a hill, but we slept in tents on the icy ground. It was one of the coldest winters in European history, and I remember the biting chill.
origin: Eventually, we arrived in Le Havre, France, and boarded freight trains known as "forty and eights." I learned later that the name referred to the capacity of the cars—forty men or eight horses. We were packed in tightly, and after a long journey, we were unloaded in Belgium. There, we were taken to a chateau on a hill, but we slept in tents on the icy ground. It was one of the coldest winters in European history, and I remember the biting chill.

text: The chow line stretched on forever, but the smell of that watery stew always brought a strange comfort. It wasn't gourmet, but after a long march, that tin cup felt like holding gold, warming my hands as much as my insides. I can still feel the dents in the metal, pressed against my lips, a taste of survival.

I remember the smell of stale coffee and mimeograph fluid hanging heavy in the air. It was always busy, a constant hum of typewriters and shouted orders echoing through the cramped space. Getting those travel requests approved felt like pulling teeth back then, but we all knew they were working hard to get us where we needed to be.

The smell of stale cigarettes and machine oil still clings to my memories of that place. We were just kids, scared and homesick, huddled together in the freezing barracks. But we were brothers, bound by something stronger than blood.
The Channel crossing was rough, I recall that much. Utah Beach wasn't much better, but at least the sand was solid under my boots. We were green, but we had a job to do, and we did it.

The old wooden pews always smelled of lemon polish and hymn books. Sunlight streamed through the stained glass, painting the dust motes dancing in the air like tiny angels. It was a place of quiet reflection, where worries seemed to fade with the organ's gentle hum.

I still remember the smell of salt and diesel on those mornings at Parris Island. The drill instructors were tough, but they were forging us into something unbreakable. Even now, the sound of a bugle call can bring a lump to my throat.

We raised a little hell back then, chasing skirts down by the soda shop after a ball game. Some of those boys never made it back from overseas, and the rest of us scattered like leaves in the wind. Still think about them every Memorial Day, though.

I remember a particular maneuver where the 38th Regimental Combat Team came down from Fort Washington to a picturesque stretch of coastline known for its dramatic cliffs and sweeping ocean views, near a grand estate that once belonged to a media mogul. I had the opportunity to meet notable figures like General Wyman, the 6th Army commander, and General Dean, who had been captured during the Korean War. Those interactions left a lasting impression on me.

While I was focused on my duties, there was always an underlying pressure. The pilots we worked with were students, and we were acutely aware that they were learning to operate complex machinery. During the last six months of my service, I was stationed at a base where they trained night fighters—planes that were fast and typically designed for operations at sea, often taking off and landing on short, moving platforms. The stress heightened when we faced tire shortages; it was not uncommon for a plane to blow a tire during takeoff. I remember the tension as we watched them return, often landing on one wheel, carefully managing their descent. They would talk each other down, relying on their training and teamwork, while we stood by with the crash crew, ready for any emergencies.

Reflecting on World War II, I believe history books often overlook the profound impact the war had on everyday life. The sheer scale of involvement and sacrifice is difficult to convey through written accounts or films. As a young man, I remember feeling out of place as a civilian surrounded by uniforms. Rationing was a part of life—shoes, food, gasoline, and tires were all limited. Blackouts were common; the bustling metropolis, with its iconic skyline and vibrant streets, was dimmed to protect against enemy submarines. My uncle, a chef at a renowned restaurant perched high above the city, saw it close because they couldn't risk the lights revealing their location.

After graduating, I won a scholarship to Mount Sinai Hospital in New York City, where I specialized in neurology and neurosurgery. Just as I was completing my studies, a significant global conflict erupted, marked by widespread mobilization and a call to arms that swept across nations. I was called to join a unit heading overseas, where the atmosphere was charged with urgency and uncertainty. I remember boarding the ship, the Pastura, a former elegant cruise liner now repurposed for military purposes, its luxurious amenities stripped away to accommodate the needs of the war effort. We were crammed into staterooms meant for two, with eleven nurses sharing one, a reflection of the desperate circumstances. The journey across the ocean was nerve-wracking; we had to keep everything dark to avoid detection by enemy forces lurking beneath the waves. After eleven long days, filled with anxiety and anticipation, we landed in Casablanca, a strategic point in the conflict, and from there, we were transported by train to Mateur, where we set up our camp in what had once been a French military training facility, now repurposed for the ongoing struggle.

In 1960, during the Cuban Missile Crisis, my brother, who was in the Air Force, encouraged me to see if I could transfer to the Air Force. I went to their base, and they welcomed me with open arms, provided I could pass the physical. I did, and I found my new role immensely enjoyable. As the base transportation officer at Bakalar Air Force Base in Columbus, Indiana, I was in charge of the motor pool, overseeing 22 mechanics and about 24 drivers. We provided taxi and bus services and ensured that all vehicles were operational. It was a fulfilling assignment, especially during a time when many of my drivers and mechanics were highly educated individuals who had joined the reserves to serve their country, often facing the moral dilemmas of military engagement and the societal pressures of a deeply divided nation. They were committed to supporting the war effort while seeking to balance their civilian lives, reflecting the complex realities of a generation caught in the turmoil of conflict and protest.

Just before the sudden and shocking attack that would thrust the nation into war, he wrote to us, excitedly sharing that he would soon be discharged after completing his service. In 1960, during the Cuban Missile Crisis, my brother encouraged me to explore a transfer to a branch of the military known for its focus on aviation and strategic defense. I visited their base, and they welcomed me with open arms, provided I could pass the physical. I did, and I found my new role immensely enjoyable. As the transportation officer at Bakalar Base in Columbus, Indiana, I was in charge of the motor pool, overseeing 22 mechanics and about 24 drivers. We provided taxi and bus services for personnel and ensured that all vehicles were operational, including those used for transporting high-ranking officials and essential supplies. It was a fulfilling assignment, especially during the Vietnam War, when many of my drivers and mechanics were highly educated individuals who had joined the reserves to serve their country while avoiding the draft, contributing to the nation's air superiority and logistical capabilities.

text: We were out on an outpost when the MPs arrived, and I felt a wave of anxiety wash over me. Thankfully, they didn’t. They took him away, explaining that his mother had sent a letter stating he was too young to be here. He looked barely out of his teens, with a boyish face that still held traces of innocence, and his uniform hung loosely on his slight frame. He protested, claiming she was lying, but they had proof—his birth certificate. They took him off, leaving me behind, and I felt a mix of relief and concern.

text: Throughout my time in England, I forged lasting friendships with the guys in my outfit. We’ve been holding reunions since 1974, with the most recent one right here in Chattanooga. After my 30-day leave, I went back to Camp Evans before heading down to a fascinating medical facility. It was located right in the heart of the city, built on what had once been a French headquarters. At Camp Stewart, I trained on a large caliber anti-aircraft artillery system designed to intercept enemy aircraft. They selected me and a few others based on our intelligence test scores to work in the director section, where we operated a sophisticated Sperry gyroscope mechanical computer. This device calculated various factors like altitude and wind to help track fast-moving enemy planes and direct our fire with precision. After completing my training, I returned home for a week before being shipped to Camp Hahn, California, where I was assigned to another artillery battalion equipped with similar heavy weaponry. I remember the luxury of working in an air-conditioned trailer while others were outside in the elements, loading shells and preparing the guns for action. We were gearing up for deployment to the Pacific, and I thought how wonderful it would be to be stationed on tropical islands.

We had lingered too long and ended up near several significant water bodies, including one notorious for its brutal battles in freezing temperatures and treacherous terrain. While others received headlines for their fierce confrontations there, we were engaged in intense fighting at different locations, and we lost almost our entire outfit.

text: The enemy had their own weapons, including a 12.5 millimeter anti-aircraft gun, which was comparable to our .50 caliber, but they rarely used them against us. I remember one night, there was a makeshift settlement nearby, about five or six miles away from us, where families who had fled their homes sought safety. The inhabitants were unarmed, with only three guards present to protect them. The North Vietnamese forces moved in, set the place ablaze, and killed several people. We watched from a distance as helicopter gunships arrived to provide support, firing at the attackers while the frightened residents, desperate to defend their temporary refuge, returned fire with their 12.5 millimeter guns.

We watched from a distance as the helicopter gunships arrived to provide support, shooting at the attackers while the refugees returned fire with their 12.5 millimeter guns.

text: Reflecting on the past, I can’t help but think about the pain that many in communities like Tell City must have felt during the tumultuous times of the world wars. My grandparents spoke German, and they instilled in my father the importance of embracing our American identity. They would remind him, “You’re in America; we speak English.” My father, born in 1883, knew very few German words, and while he never served in the military, one of his brothers did fight in a significant conflict that shaped our nation. Our family has a long history of service; my great-uncle James Varner fought bravely in a bitter struggle that divided the country, marked by intense battles and deep societal rifts, and he never returned home.

It was a much-needed break from the harsh realities of war, and I felt grateful to experience a moment of joy amidst the turmoil.

It was a stark introduction to my new life in a place where the heat was relentless, the landscape was a mix of dense jungles and muddy terrain, and the uncertainty of each day loomed large.

The smell of turned earth always takes me back. Even now, I can feel the sun on my neck and the ache in my hands, pushing that old plow. It was honest work, dawn 'til dusk, feeding families and building a life from the soil.

The sets we prepared were crucial supplies, packed in boxes with various parts that would be needed in the field.

One incident that stands out in my memory occurred while we were moving equipment down a steep hill in Germany. The brakes failed, and we careened down the slope, colliding with a makeshift wagon filled with belongings—tattered blankets, worn-out clothing, and a few cherished items hastily gathered. A group of weary individuals, their faces etched with hardship and uncertainty, stood nearby, their eyes wide with shock. It was a tragic accident, and some people were hurt. We felt terrible about it, and it was a stark reminder of the unpredictability of our situation. These experiences, both joyful and sorrowful, shaped my time in Germany and left lasting impressions on me.

text: After graduating, I won a scholarship to Mount Sinai Hospital in New York City, where I specialized in the study of the nervous system and intricate surgical procedures involving the brain and spinal cord. My training included mastering delicate techniques to address traumatic injuries, tumors, and neurological disorders, requiring precision and a deep understanding of complex anatomy. But just as I was finishing my studies, World War II broke out, and I was asked to join a unit heading overseas. I remember boarding the ship, the Pastura, a former elegant cruise liner now repurposed for the war. We were crammed into staterooms meant for two, with eleven nurses sharing one. The journey across the ocean was nerve-wracking; we had to keep everything dark to avoid enemy submarines. We landed in Casablanca after eleven long days, and from there, we were transported by train to Mateur, where we set up our camp in what had once been a French military training facility.

text: The bocage was hell on earth, hedgerows taller than a man on horseback. I can still taste the dust and the cordite, hear the rumble of the Shermans struggling through the mud. We were told to take it, no matter the cost, and God, the cost was high.

Back then, you knew who was really in charge by the sheer weight of metal on their shoulders. The respect they commanded wasn't just given, it was earned in blood and sweat across continents. Even now, I can still picture that cluster of stars, burning bright against the olive drab.

I remember the endless nights, the hum of the equipment, the green glow on my face. We were always chasing something, a ghost in the machine, trying to make sense of the chaos. Sometimes, the silence after a successful intercept was the loudest thing of all.

The heat was always oppressive, clinging to you like a wet blanket. We fought for every inch of black sand on those volcanic beaches, a brutal island hopping campaign in the Pacific. Sometimes, at night, I can still hear the echoes of gunfire and the cries of the wounded carried on the humid breeze.

However, my journey wasn’t without its hurdles. I vividly recall the day I failed my first physical due to my eyesight. It was disheartening, but I was determined. I went back to the doctor and asked for a waiver. He told me I didn’t even have to serve, but I insisted, “I want to go.” When I expressed my desire to fight against those who oppress their own people, enforce strict control over their citizens, and threaten the stability of the region, he nodded in understanding. That moment solidified my resolve to join the military.

In 1960, during the Cuban Missile Crisis, my brother, who was in the Air Force, encouraged me to see if I could transfer to the service. I visited their base, and they welcomed me with open arms, provided I could pass the physical. I did, and I found my new role immensely enjoyable. As the transportation officer at Bakalar Air Force Base in Columbus, Indiana, I oversaw a team of 22 mechanics and about 24 drivers. We managed a fleet of vehicles, ensuring they were always ready for duty, whether for official travel, troop transport, or logistical support. Our responsibilities included routine maintenance, repairs, and inspections to keep everything in top condition. It was a fulfilling assignment, especially during the Vietnam War, when many of my drivers and mechanics were highly educated individuals who had joined the reserves to serve their country while avoiding the draft.

The news came over the radio, a crackling voice cutting through the humid summer air. We poured out of the barracks, a wave of uniforms celebrating in the streets of Manila. I remember thinking of the boys who wouldn't be coming home, even as our cheers echoed into the night.

Every year, Uncle Sam would poke and prod us like prize cattle. I always dreaded the eye exam, hoping my vision hadn't gotten any worse. It was all part of making sure we were "fit for duty," ready to serve our country.

So, I made the decision to submit a 1049, a request to go to Vietnam. I was ready for action, eager to serve in a way that felt meaningful. When I returned home on leave after about a year of rigorous training and discipline, the atmosphere was starting to shift. While my family was proud of my accomplishments, especially my training in surgical specialties, the general sentiment toward those in uniform was changing. It was no longer popular to be serving in a structured environment where camaraderie and sacrifice were the norms. People felt sympathy for those who were drafted into a conflict, and if you volunteered, well, they thought there was something off about you, as if you were seeking out danger or glory in a time of turmoil.

text: The lake wind carried the scent of industry and fish, a familiar perfume from my childhood. Summers were spent dodging the spray from the breakers down by the docks, the clang of the shipyard a constant background rhythm. Even now, I can almost taste the greasy burgers from that little stand near the tracks.

The air always hummed there, a low thrum you felt in your teeth more than heard. Banks of lights painted the room in a perpetual twilight, and the smell of ozone clung to your uniform like a second skin. We watched those screens, every flicker a potential life or death decision.

The canteen always smelled faintly of machine oil and stale coffee, no matter how many times we scrubbed it down. We'd sit there, shoulders touching, not saying much, just the clink of mess kits and the distant rumble that reminded us all too well of what we'd seen. Sometimes, late at night, I still hear the echoes of those days in my dreams.

We were based down in Italy then, chasing the Axis out of Africa and onwards. Some of the boys were transferred from the 31st, others straight from the states - a real mixed bag. Flew mostly P-40s, though I heard whispers of Mustangs later in the war.

We trained hard in '58, B-52s screaming overhead day and night. The town wasn't much back then, just dust devils and a few hardy souls eking out a living. They were glad to have us, though; brought a bit of life, and a whole lot of noise, to that West Texas plain.

We landed in Marseille, and from there it was right into the thick of it. Snow-covered mountains and dense forests... I still remember the chill that went right through you as we pushed toward the Siegfried Line. Some of the best men I ever knew never made it back from that campaign.

Back then, it was just a dusty crossroads, nothing special. But the general knew its importance, the way it sat on the supply lines. We dug in deep, knowing what was coming, and prayed our luck would hold.

I remember the day I decided to become a nurse. As a child, I was always the one running around the neighborhood, rescuing fallen birds and bandaging up my friends when they scraped their knees. I found joy in caring for others, even if it was just a simple cut or a little bird that needed help. When the Great Depression hit and my father lost everything, my mother suggested nursing as a career. It seemed like a perfect fit, so we looked into it. The best hospital in New York State at the time was located in a charming town nestled along the banks of the Hudson River, known for its picturesque views and historic architecture. It was a bit of a journey from our home on Long Island, but we had a summer house not far from there, surrounded by lush greenery and the gentle sounds of nature. I applied, got accepted, and spent three wonderful years learning and growing under the guidance of the black Franciscans, a highly educated order of nuns.

The peace movement was in full swing, and the atmosphere was filled with the counterculture of the time—hippies, protests, and a general sense of rebellion. The memories of dense jungles, sweltering heat, and the sounds of distant gunfire were still fresh in my mind. It was a stark contrast to the carefree life I remembered before I left, where the only battles were those of everyday life, not survival.

It was a stark contrast to the fun-filled world I remembered before I left.

text: As we continued our advance, we reached the Elbe River, where we prepared for an assault crossing. We loaded into small wooden boats, and as we paddled across, we were met with machine gun fire and some artillery. I remember one moment vividly when shrapnel struck one of our jeeps, causing the canvas cover to catch fire. Inside, we had heavy, cylindrical shells packed tightly, each one designed to be launched from a tube, their explosive potential a constant reminder of the danger we faced. We all scrambled to put it out, fearing an explosion that could have been catastrophic. After crossing the river, we set up a defensive position on the right flank of the bridgehead, where we spent just one night.

They were mostly in their early twenties and approached their training with a serious demeanor, fully aware of the stakes involved. Thankfully, we didn’t have to fight our way ashore; the infantry and combat engineers had already secured the beachhead. Just before the attack on Pearl Harbor, he wrote to us, excitedly sharing that he would soon be discharged after fulfilling his service. My training began at Fort Benjamin Harrison, just outside Indianapolis, Indiana, where I spent a couple of weeks before being sent to Selfridge Field in Michigan. There, I joined a fighter squadron known for its distinguished history and exceptional performance in various campaigns. Uniquely, I remained with that same unit from day one until my discharge in June 1945. It was rare for someone to stay with the same group for that long, but I was fortunate. We worked on the same planes, including the iconic P-47 Thunderbolt, forming bonds that would last a lifetime as we flew countless missions together, supporting ground troops and engaging in dogfights.

This time, the shock of returning wasn’t as intense, but I was just so glad to be getting out. I had considered staying in longer because I didn’t mind it; I had a decent job and could have built a career in that structured environment. I could have ended up in a facility in San Antonio, surrounded by fellow service members, or even overseas in England, living in barracks and following a strict routine. But by the time I was leaving, my pride in my role had diminished. Initially, I was proud of my service and what I was doing, donning my uniform and following orders, but as the conflict progressed and we began handing over responsibilities to the locals, it felt like a joke. The focus had shifted from winning battles to merely surviving day-to-day, and I didn’t want to be part of that anymore.

text: One of my most memorable experiences in the Air Force took place during our monthly training activities, where we simulated high-stakes scenarios that tested our readiness and teamwork. Living on a small island like Guam meant we regularly faced the threat of tornadoes and hurricanes. Despite the severe weather warnings, we had to volunteer to move logistical supplies outside to the flight line where the planes were parked, preparing for potential missions. It didn’t matter if a storm was brewing; we had to brave the elements, ensuring everything was in place for rapid deployment. Those moments were tough, but they forged a strong bond among us as we worked together under pressure, honing our skills for real-world challenges.
